title: Today's AI Brief: Voice AI Systems Are Vulnerable to Hidden Audio Attacks
summary: A look at the latest AI news, including voice AI vulnerabilities and a new cloud infrastructure player.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Voice AI Systems Are Vulnerable to Hidden Audio Attacks
imageCaption: Voice AI Systems Are Vulnerable to Hidden Audio Attacks
imageSourceTitle: Voice AI Systems Are Vulnerable to Hidden Audio Attacks

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and I'm here to walk you through some of the latest happenings in the world of AI. First up, let's talk about voice AI systems. They're everywhere - from your smart speaker at home to customer service bots. But, it turns out, they're not as secure as we'd like to think.

Researchers have found that AI-powered voice and audio tools are vulnerable to hidden audio attacks. This is due to advances in large audio-language models, or LALMs, which can analyze and generate audio. The concern is that these systems can be tricked into doing things they shouldn't, like revealing sensitive information or performing unintended actions. It's a bit like someone whispering a secret to your smart speaker, and it just happens to obey.

In other news, Railway, a San Francisco-based cloud platform, has secured $100 million in funding to challenge the likes of AWS with AI-native cloud infrastructure. What's interesting here is that Railway has managed to amass two million developers without spending a dime on marketing. That's like getting a huge audience without buying a single ad - it's not something you see every day.

The funding round brings Railway's total funding to date to over $150 million. The company plans to use the funds to expand its platform and take on established players in the cloud infrastructure space. It's a bold move, and we'll be keeping an eye on how it plays out.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
